segments.text: Arya: John, how is it going with your research on water hyacinth?
John: Well, I went on a field trip on my own and collected the primary data.
John: After I came back, I also referred to the published data.
Arya: So what do you know about this plant?
John: Water hyacinth grows in tropical countries.
John: It has beautiful purple-blue flowers, but everybody hates it.
Arya: Why is that?
John: Water hyacinth, as the world's worst invasive aquatic plant, has attracted significant attention.
John: Millions of these plants grow in rivers and lakes and have brought huge environmental and cultural problems to the local people.
John: Farmers cannot travel in boats on the water, and thus cannot fish in it.
John: In addition, heavy free-floating mats of water hyacinth are clogging the dam at the power station in West Africa.
John: Turbines of the power station are often shut down because of the intertwined leaves.
John: Dams often cease to function as a result, severely restricting the electricity generation.
John: Dense mats of water hyacinth also shade the surface and prevent algae growth, thus limiting the production of oxygen and threatening the survival of fish.
Arya: So where did water hyacinth originate from?
John: Water hyacinth is native to Amazon Basin in Latin America.
John: It has spread mainly to the tropics and subtropics throughout much of the world since the 1800s.
John: Later, it was introduced into Europe and Africa as an ornamental garden pond plant due to its beauty.
John: Flowers of this plant were being sold in local markets in African countries as a source of income for women.
John: Since the 1900s, this weed has infested lakes, rivers and creeks of the delta areas in West Africa.
Arya: I guess whoever introduced this plant had never expected the disastrous impact it would have on the local ecological environment.
Arya: What kind of habitat does water hyacinth thrive most in?
John: Well, it has been discovered that the nutrient-rich environment contributes significantly to the rapid proliferation of this weed, which obtains its nutrients directly from the water.
John: This is normally compounded with a decrease of nutrients along the river banks.
Arya: Why is that?
John: The main reason for soil nutrient loss is that local residents cut down trees.
John: Excessive deforestation accelerates soil erosion, which indirectly compounds this issue.
John: Without enough trees to retain nutrients in the soil, frequent rain might partly aggravate this by carrying some of the nutrients into the waterways.
Arya: Are there any effective measures to control or even eradicate this weed?
John: Yes, a number of approaches have been widely applied throughout the world.
John: Among them, biological control is the most widely favoured long-term control method by introducing a type of insect that feeds on water hyacinth.
John: So far, Nigeria has used this method for 6 months.
John: However, water hyacinth won't be removed immediately.

John: The side effects of this method on local ecology will remain unforeseen for years because it takes time for this external insect species to reach a density sufficient enough to affect ecological development.
Arya: Then what about other approaches?
Arya: Is there a quicker way to control it?
John: Well, the mechanical removal of water hyacinth is seen as the best short-term solution.
John: This option includes harvesting plants and in-site cutting.
John: This method can immediately open physical space for fish and boat traffic.
John: It is, however, inefficient for workers to process extensive areas, and costly to use large cutting and dredging equipment.
John: Ironically, it costs even more to dispose of this plant than to remove it.
Arya: Can we make use of this weed and turn it into something good?
John: Now scientists think that water hyacinth can be useful.
John: The plant itself, although more than 95% water, has a fibrous tissue and a high energy and protein content, and can be used for a variety of useful applications.
Arya: So what do people do with water hyacinth that is initially removed?
John: First, the cut-down water hyacinth is left to dry under the sun for a few days before being mixed with ash and animal manure.
John: The mixture can be used as alternative soil rather than fertiliser.
John: It can grow crops with increased yield.
Arya: That sounds interesting.
John: Mushroom farmers can benefit from water hyacinth as well.
John: Using the mixture mentioned earlier, mushrooms generally grow much faster so that farmers can harvest mushrooms sooner, which enables them to make money faster.
Arya: I see.
Arya: It can be used to grow oyster and straw mushrooms, right?
John: Yes.
John: In India, attempts have been made to grow these types of edible mushrooms organically utilizing water hyacinth.
John: These mushrooms provide sufficient nutritional values including minerals and high-quality protein which are essential to people's level of fitness.
Arya: Right.
Arya: Are there any other measures taken to utilise water hyacinth?
John: In South-east Asia, people feed cows using chopped water hyacinth mixed with other vegetables.
John: Then the waste from cows is treated to produce methane gas, which can be used as fuel for cooking, lighting or powering an engine.
John: And with global warming getting worse, developing alternative sources of energy will be more urgent than ever.
Arya: Maybe in the future people will love water hyacinth instead of hating it.
John: Yes, that's very likely if we can make full use of this weed.
